section.title: Do Not Keep Silent, O God
section.sectionRationale: The last of the Asaphite psalms in Book III, this urgent communal imprecation names a ten-nation coalition conspiring to erase Israel's very name, then appeals to God's past victories over Midian and Sisera, asking Him to act again so that all the earth will know He alone is Most High.
section.thesis: When enemies unite to erase God's people from history, the honest, Spirit-given prayer of God's people is not silent resignation but a bold plea for God to act as He has before, so that even His enemies would come to know that He alone is the LORD, Most High over all the earth.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the urgent cry for God's attention, 1-2; the roll call of conspiring nations, 3-8; the plea for God to repeat past victories, 9-12; and the prayer for the enemies' humiliation to end in the whole earth knowing the LORD, 13-18.

units.label: A cry for God's attention
units.note: be not silent, be not still

verses.text: O God, be not {{charash|silent}}; be not speechless; be not still, O God.
See how Your enemies rage, how Your foes have reared their heads.

units.label: The alliance of enemies
units.note: a coalition to erase Israel's name

verses.text: With cunning they {{sod|scheme}} against Your people and conspire against those You cherish,
saying, "Come, let us erase them as a nation; may the name of Israel be remembered no more."
For with one mind they plot together; they form an alliance against You,
the tents of Edom and the Ishmaelites, of Moab and the Hagrites,
of Gebal, Ammon, and Amalek, of Philistia with the people of Tyre.
Even Assyria has joined them, lending strength to the sons of Lot. Selah

units.label: A plea to repeat past victories
units.note: as You did to Midian and Sisera

verses.text: Do to them as You did to Midian, as to Sisera and Jabin at the River Kishon,
who perished at Endor and became like dung on the ground.
Make their nobles like Oreb and Zeeb, and all their princes like Zebah and Zalmunna,
who said, "Let us possess for ourselves the pastures of God."

units.label: That they may know You alone are Most High
units.note: the goal of judgment is God's name known

verses.text: Make them like tumbleweed, O my God, like chaff before the wind.
As fire consumes a forest, as a flame sets the mountains ablaze,
so pursue them with Your tempest, and terrify them with Your storm.
Cover their faces with shame, that they may seek Your name, O LORD.
May they be ever ashamed and terrified; may they perish in disgrace.
May they know that You alone, whose name is the LORD, are {{elyon|Most High}} over all the earth.

charash.gloss: To be silent, deaf, or motionless. The opening triple plea, 'be not silent, be not speechless, be not still,' uses forms of this same root to press God, with mounting urgency, to act rather than stay quiet.

sod.gloss: A council, secret plan, or conspiracy. The enemy nations do not merely happen to oppose Israel; they hold sod, a deliberate, unified council, against God's own cherished people, verse 3.

elyon.gloss: Most High, a name stressing God's supremacy over every rival power, human or spiritual. The psalm's final goal is not merely Israel's safety but that the nations themselves would come to know the LORD is elyon, Most High over all the earth.

basic.context: Ten nations, Edom, the Ishmaelites, Moab, the Hagrites, Gebal, Ammon, Amalek, Philistia, Tyre, and Assyria, unite in a plot to erase Israel's very name from history. Asaph pleads urgently for God not to stay silent, recalls His past victories over Midian and Sisera, and asks Him to act again, not merely for Israel's safety but so that all the earth would know the LORD alone is Most High.
basic.hermeneutics: This is an honest, Spirit-given imprecatory prayer, not personal vengeance; the psalmist brings real danger and real anger before God rather than taking matters into his own hands, and asks for judgment that leads even enemies to seek God's name.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens by piling up forms of charash, silent or still, pressing God to act. The nations' plot is called a sod, a deliberate council or conspiracy, against God's own people. The final goal is that all would know God is elyon, Most High, over the whole earth, not merely over Israel.
basic.typology: The pattern of many nations uniting against God and His people, only to be answered by God's decisive intervention, anticipates the final gathering of the nations against God's Anointed described in Psalm 2 and fulfilled in the opposition and vindication of Christ, Acts 4:25-27.
basic.christ: Acts 4:25-27 shows the early church reading Psalm 2's picture of raging, allied nations as fulfilled in the opposition Herod, Pilate, and the nations mounted against Jesus at the cross, an opposition God overturned in the resurrection, just as this psalm asks God to overturn Israel's enemies.
basic.god: God is shown as the one who can be pleaded with honestly in the face of real danger, whose judgment on His enemies is not an end in itself but aims at their knowing His name, and who alone is Most High over every power that conspires against Him.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 2 previews the nations raging against God's Anointed, fulfilled in Acts 4:25-27. 2 Chronicles 20 records a strikingly similar coalition against Jehoshaphat, answered by God's deliverance. Romans 8:31 answers this psalm's fear with settled confidence, if God is for us, who can be against us.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's plea against a coalition bent on erasing God's people previews the pattern fulfilled at the cross, where the nations conspired against God's Anointed, only to be answered by God's decisive vindication in the resurrection of Christ.

context.literary: A tightly built imprecatory psalm: an urgent opening cry, 1-2; a detailed roll call of ten conspiring nations, 3-8; a historical appeal to past victories, 9-12; and a closing plea whose goal is the nations' own knowledge of God, 13-18.
context.historical: Many conservative readings place this psalm during King Jehoshaphat's reign, when Moab, Ammon, and others formed a coalition against Judah, 2 Chronicles 20, a crisis answered by God's deliverance through worship rather than battle.
context.canonical: The last psalm attributed to Asaph in the Psalter's numbering here, closing Book III's cluster of communal laments with a plea that judgment itself would lead the nations to seek the LORD's name.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the real, present danger of a united enemy coalition honestly before God, to appeal to His past faithfulness in deliverance, and to ask that His judgment on Israel's enemies would ultimately lead even them to acknowledge Him as Most High over all the earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical threat and a specific list of enemy nations; it prescribes as an abiding pattern that God's people may bring danger and desired justice honestly to God in prayer, trusting Him rather than taking vengeance into their own hands.

debates.question: How should believers today pray the imprecations of verses 9-17, asking for enemies to be shamed, terrified, and destroyed?

views.label: Pray them as they stand, entrusting all vengeance to God rather than acting on it personally, since the psalm itself models bringing anger to God rather than suppressing or acting on it
views.case: The New Testament repeats this pattern, Romans 12:19, leave room for God's wrath, and the psalm's own goal, verse 16, is that enemies would seek God's name, not simply suffer.

views.label: Pray them transformed through Christ's teaching to love enemies, redirecting the psalm's honest desire for justice toward a hope for the enemies' repentance and salvation rather than their destruction
views.case: Jesus commands love and prayer for enemies, Matthew 5:44, so New Testament prayer may take up this psalm's honesty about evil while aiming its hope explicitly at repentance rather than ruin.

debates.bearing: Both approaches agree the psalm licenses honest, unedited prayer about real danger and real evil, entrusted fully to God rather than to personal retaliation.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm opens by stacking forms of charash, silence or stillness, urging God to act. The enemies' plan is a sod, a deliberate conspiracy, against God's own people. The ultimate goal named at the close is that all would know God is elyon, Most High, not only over Israel but over the whole earth.

typology.type: Many nations uniting in conspiracy against God and His people, verses 3-8
typology.fulfillment: The gathering of nations against God's Anointed, foreseen in Psalm 2 and fulfilled at the cross, Acts 4:25-27, and overturned in the resurrection.

study.christ: The pattern this psalm laments, many nations conspiring together against God's own people, is the same pattern Psalm 2 foresees against God's Anointed King, a pattern the early church saw fulfilled in the combined opposition of Herod, Pilate, the Gentiles, and the peoples of Israel against Jesus, Acts 4:25-27. Just as this psalm asks God to act decisively rather than stay silent, God answered the conspiracy against His Son with the vindication of the resurrection.
study.god: God is the one who can be approached honestly with real fear and real anger at conspiring evil, whose justice aims not merely at Israel's safety but at making His own supreme name known to every nation, and who alone deserves the title Most High over the whole earth.

crossReferences.note: Previews nations raging together against God's Anointed, the same pattern this psalm laments against Israel.
The early church reads the raging of the nations as fulfilled in the opposition against Jesus at the cross.
Records a strikingly similar coalition against Judah under Jehoshaphat, answered by God's deliverance.

study.oneStory: Psalm 83 pleads honestly with God against a coalition determined to erase His people, a pattern of nations conspiring against God and His own that culminates in the united opposition to Christ at the cross, and finds its answer in the same way, God's decisive, vindicating action in the resurrection.

quiz.q: What is the psalmist's urgent opening request in verses 1-2?

options.text: That God would not be silent, speechless, or still while enemies rage against Him.
options.note: Verses 1-2 make this triple-stacked plea directly.

options.text: That God would remain completely uninvolved.
options.note: The opposite is requested; the psalmist begs God to act, not to stay uninvolved.

options.text: That the enemies would be left completely alone.
options.note: The plea is for God's active intervention against the conspiring enemies, not for their being left alone.

options.text: That Israel would take matters into its own hands.
options.note: The prayer is directed entirely to God's action, not to Israel acting independently.

quiz.q: What is the stated goal of the conspiring nations, verse 4?

options.text: To erase Israel as a nation so its name would be remembered no more.
options.note: Verse 4 states this goal explicitly in the enemies' own words.

options.text: To form a peaceful trade agreement with Israel.
options.note: The stated goal is Israel's total erasure, not peaceful trade.

options.text: To convert to worship of the LORD.
options.note: The nations' goal here is destruction, though the psalm later hopes some might come to seek God's name, verse 16.

options.text: To simply raise their own borders slightly.
options.note: The stated aim is Israel's complete erasure as a nation, not a minor border adjustment.

quiz.q: What past victories does the psalmist ask God to repeat, verses 9-11?

options.text: God's victories over Midian, and Sisera and Jabin at the River Kishon.
options.note: Verses 9-11 name these specific historical deliverances.

options.text: The victory at the Red Sea over Pharaoh.
options.note: The psalm names Midian, Sisera, and Jabin specifically, not the Red Sea crossing.

options.text: No past victories are mentioned at all.
options.note: The psalm explicitly recalls specific past deliverances by name.

options.text: The building of Solomon's temple.
options.note: The psalm recalls military deliverances from Judges, not temple construction.

quiz.q: What is the ultimate purpose of the judgment the psalmist requests, verse 16 and verse 18?

options.text: That the enemies would be covered with shame so that they seek God's name, and that all would know the LORD alone is Most High over the earth.
options.note: Verses 16 and 18 state this purpose directly.

options.text: Personal revenge for the psalmist alone, with no wider purpose.
options.note: The stated purpose is the nations' knowledge of God's name, not mere personal revenge.

options.text: The complete destruction of all nations without any further purpose.
options.note: The psalm names a purpose beyond destruction, namely, that they would seek and know God's name.

options.text: The expansion of Israel's borders for economic gain.
options.note: The stated purpose is theological, God's name known, not economic expansion.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's pattern of nations conspiring together connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It matches the pattern of nations raging against God's Anointed, foreseen in Psalm 2 and fulfilled in the opposition to Jesus at the cross, Acts 4:25-27.
options.note: Acts 4:25-27 applies this exact pattern of united opposition to the crucifixion of Jesus.

options.text: It has no connection to any New Testament event.
options.note: The early church directly connects this pattern of conspiring nations to the opposition against Christ.

options.text: It predicts the exact modern nations that would oppose Israel today.
options.note: The psalm names specific ancient nations; its New Testament fulfillment concerns opposition to Christ, not a modern political forecast.

options.text: It only concerns commercial trade disputes.
options.note: The conflict described is a military and existential threat, not a trade dispute.

questions: The psalmist boldly asks God not to stay silent or still, verses 1-2. What situation are you facing where you need to bring the same bold, honest urgency to God in prayer?

The enemies unite 'with one mind' against God's people, verse 5. Where do you see coordinated opposition to what is good in your own life or community, and how does this psalm shape your prayer for it?
The psalmist grounds his hope in what God has done before, Midian, Sisera, verses 9-11. What has God already done in your life or in Scripture that you can appeal to in your own current fears?
The psalm's goal is not mere defeat of enemies but that they would 'seek Your name,' verse 16. How does this shape the way you pray about people who oppose or oppose you?
The psalm ends with the hope that all the earth would know the LORD alone is Most High, verse 18. How does this global, missionary hope change the way you think about your own trials and conflicts?

translationNotes.note: 'Be not speechless' can also be rendered 'be not deaf,' both readings pressing the same urgent plea that God actively respond rather than remain unmoved.

The ten named peoples, Edom, Ishmaelites, Moab, Hagrites, Gebal, Ammon, Amalek, Philistia, Tyre, and Assyria, represent a strikingly broad and otherwise unlikely coalition, underscoring how seriously the psalmist takes this threat to Israel's survival.
'Most High' renders elyon, a name emphasizing God's supremacy over every rival power; the psalm's final aim is that this name be known 'over all the earth,' not Israel's territory alone.

Footnote reference marker in verse 1 removed; one em dash at the end of verse 5 converted to a comma. Verse count (18) and first/last verse wording checked against the section map. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) could be confirmed specifically on Psalm 83 this session, so sermon status is 'none'.
